content.text: Every agent built in Python starts with naming values and understanding what kind of data each value is.
Variables bind names to objects in memory; data types determine which operations are valid and how values behave at system boundaries.
Getting this foundation right prevents the silent bugs that corrupt conversation state, break tool calls, and produce invalid API payloads.
is a name that refers to an object stored in memory. The assignment operator
creates or rebinds that reference — it does not copy the object unless you explicitly request a copy. Python is
: the type is an attribute of the object itself, not of the name. The same name can refer to an integer now and a string later, though that flexibility is rarely a good idea in practice.
classifies an object and defines both its structure and the set of operations it supports. At runtime, every object carries its type as an attribute accessible via
. The interpreter consults the type before dispatching any operation — arithmetic, comparison, concatenation — and raises
Core built-in types for agent code
— text: prompts, model output, JSON serialized as text, tool names
— whole numbers: token counts, retry limits, list indices, pagination offsets
— decimals: temperature, top_p, confidence scores, latency measurements
: feature flags, guard conditions, stream toggles
— sentinel for "no value": optional fields, missing tool results, unset config
— raw binary: file content, image data, cryptographic material
— complex numbers; rarely used in agent code but included for completeness
— each hold other objects. They are covered in depth in the Collections topic; this topic focuses on the scalar primitives and the variable binding model that containers also rely on.

Agent systems constantly move data across boundaries: HTTP responses, environment variables, JSON payloads, database rows, and in-memory state all arrive as strings or raw bytes, then must be converted to the right Python types before use. A string token count (
) behaves entirely differently from an integer (
). Adding one to the other raises
silently. These subtle mismatches break logic without raising obvious errors.

Understanding Python's reference model — that assignment creates a reference, not a copy — prevents a different class of bugs: shared mutable state across agent turns, configuration objects mutated by one part of the system and read unexpectedly by another.

Dynamic typing means mistakes surface at runtime, not at parse time. Knowing the types flowing through your code lets you write defensive checks and explicit conversions at the right boundaries, making agent pipelines reliable even when model output is unpredictable.
— knowing mutability prevents accidental shared-state bugs across tool calls and request handlers
quickly expose shape mismatches during development
— LLM APIs expect specific JSON types; Python values must match exactly before serialization
— explicit conversion at system boundaries keeps internal logic clean and consistent
— static analysis tools like mypy and pyright work best when types are consistent and predictable
vs. runtime surprises when a variable's type changes under the same name across function calls
— type errors surface during execution unless you add type hints and run a static checker
— two names can refer to the same mutable object; changes through one name are visible through the other
— empty strings, zero integers, and empty containers are all falsy, which is convenient but can mask bugs when checking for "no value"
uses IEEE 754 double precision; for exact decimal arithmetic (money, token pricing) use
CPython maintains a namespace — a dictionary mapping names to objects. When you write
object with value 4096 in memory, then inserts an entry
→ (reference to that int) in the current namespace. The int object itself carries its type (
), its value, and a reference count used by the garbage collector.

again — does not modify the existing int object. It creates a new int object (8192) and updates the namespace entry to point at the new object. The old object's reference count drops by one; if it reaches zero, CPython frees it.

# Name binding and rebinding
max_tokens = 4096
print(id(max_tokens))   # memory address of the int 4096

max_tokens = 8192
print(id(max_tokens))   # different address — new object

# Two names, one object
config_a = {"model": "gpt-4o"}
config_b = config_a          # same dict object
config_b["temperature"] = 0.2
print(config_a)              # {"model": "gpt-4o", "temperature": 0.2}
                             # both names see the change

# Immutable scalars: rebinding creates a new object
x = 10
y = x                        # y also refers to the int 10
x = 20                       # rebinding x; y still refers to 10
print(y)                     # 10 — unchanged

role = "assistant"
print(type(role))              # <class 'str'>
print(isinstance(role, str))   # True

# isinstance handles inheritance and unions
payload = {"content": "hello"}
print(isinstance(payload, dict))      # True
print(isinstance(payload, (dict, list)))  # True — tuple of types

# type() equality is fragile with subclasses — prefer isinstance
import io
buf = io.StringIO()
print(type(buf) == io.IOBase)        # False
print(isinstance(buf, io.IOBase))    # True
Names start with a letter or underscore; digits are not allowed as the first character
A leading underscore signals internal use:
Avoid shadowing built-ins: never name a variable

— cannot be changed after creation. Any operation that appears to "modify" them returns a new object. This makes them safe to share across multiple references without risk of one part of the code surprising another.

— can be modified in place. When two names refer to the same mutable object, a mutation through one name is visible through the other. This matters enormously in agent loops where conversation history dicts or tool-result lists are shared across steps.
Python rarely coerces types automatically. Instead, you call the target type as a constructor:
. Explicit conversion is the correct pattern for any data arriving from outside the Python runtime.

# Explicit conversion examples — safe patterns
raw_count = "512"           # from environment variable or JSON string
count = int(raw_count)      # 512 as int
print(count + 1)            # 513

latency_str = "0.842"
latency = float(latency_str)  # 0.842 as float
print(latency * 1000)         # 842.0 ms

# Boolean truthiness — know the falsy values
falsy_examples = [0, 0.0, "", b"", [], {}, set(), None, False]
for val in falsy_examples:
    print(repr(val), "is falsy:", not bool(val))

# Parsing bool from JSON strings — do NOT rely on truthiness
raw_flag = "false"          # string "false" is truthy!
flag = raw_flag.lower() == "true"   # False — correct
flag_wrong = bool(raw_flag)         # True — WRONG: non-empty string

# int/float interop
token_budget = 4096         # int
used_fraction = 0.3         # float
used_tokens = int(token_budget * used_fraction)   # 1228 (truncation)
remaining = token_budget - used_tokens             # 2868

# str() always works; int() and float() raise ValueError on bad input
try:
    bad = int("4o")
except ValueError as exc:
    print(f"Conversion failed: {exc}")

The diagram below shows the three-layer model that Python uses whenever you write an assignment: a namespace entry (the variable name) pointing to an object in memory, which in turn carries its type. The lower half catalogs all built-in types by mutability.
immediately when data crosses a system boundary — HTTP response, env var, CLI argument, file read, JSON parse
as a sentinel for optional values
for values that must not change during a run:
one variable name to different types within a function — it defeats static analysis and obscures intent
The right time to think about types is
you write any arithmetic or comparison involving data that arrived as a string — which is virtually always the case with LLM outputs, tool results, and API responses.

The following pattern shows how to set up agent configuration variables with correct types, validate incoming data, and safely convert at the boundary between external strings and internal Python logic.

import os
from decimal import Decimal

# -------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1. Module-level constants — immutable by convention
# -------------------------------------------------------------------
DEFAULT_MODEL = "gpt-4o"
DEFAULT_MAX_TOKENS = 1024
DEFAULT_TEMPERATURE = 0.3
MAX_RETRIES = 3
STREAM = False

# -------------------------------------------------------------------
# 2. Read configuration from environment with safe defaults and types
# -------------------------------------------------------------------
def load_agent_config() -> dict:
    """Read all agent settings from environment; validate types early."""
    raw_max_tokens = os.environ.get("MAX_TOKENS", str(DEFAULT_MAX_TOKENS))
    raw_temperature = os.environ.get("TEMPERATURE", str(DEFAULT_TEMPERATURE))
    raw_stream = os.environ.get("STREAM", "false")

    # Convert at the boundary — let bad values raise immediately
    max_tokens: int = int(raw_max_tokens)
    temperature: float = float(raw_temperature)
    stream: bool = raw_stream.strip().lower() == "true"

    return {
        "model": os.environ.get("MODEL", DEFAULT_MODEL),  # str
        "max_tokens": max_tokens,                          # int
        "temperature": temperature,                        # float
        "stream": stream,                                  # bool
    }

# -------------------------------------------------------------------
# 3. Validate a message dict coming from a tool response
# -------------------------------------------------------------------
def validate_message(msg: object) -> dict:
    """Ensure msg is a dict with the expected typed fields."""
    if not isinstance(msg, dict):
        raise TypeError(f"Expected dict, got {type(msg).__name__}")
    role = msg.get("role")
    content = msg.get("content")
    if not isinstance(role, str):
        raise TypeError(f"role must be str, got {type(role).__name__}")
    if not isinstance(content, (str, type(None))):
        raise TypeError(f"content must be str or None")
    return msg

# -------------------------------------------------------------------
# 4. Exact decimal arithmetic for pricing (float is imprecise)
# -------------------------------------------------------------------
PRICE_PER_1K_TOKENS = Decimal("0.002")   # USD per 1000 tokens

def calculate_cost(token_count: int) -> Decimal:
    return (Decimal(token_count) / 1000) * PRICE_PER_1K_TOKENS

if __name__ == "__main__":
    config = load_agent_config()
    print(config)
    cost = calculate_cost(config["max_tokens"])
    print(f"Estimated cost: ${cost:.6f}")
Valid vs. invalid operations across types

# str + str  → concatenation
greeting = "Hello, " + "agent"    # "Hello, agent"

# int + int  → arithmetic
total = 100 + 50                   # 150

# str + int  → TypeError
# "tokens: " + 100               # raises TypeError

# Correct: convert first
label = "tokens: " + str(100)     # "tokens: 100"

# bool is a subtype of int
print(True + True)                 # 2
print(True == 1)                   # True
print(False == 0)                  # True

# None comparisons — always use 'is', never '=='
result = None
if result is None:
    print("no result yet")         # correct
# if result == None:               # works but style violation

# Float precision — classic trap
print(0.1 + 0.2 == 0.3)           # False (floating-point)
print(abs(0.1 + 0.2 - 0.3) < 1e-9)  # True — correct comparison

# Walrus operator (Python 3.8+) — assign and test in one expression
import re
text = "Tool result: 42 items found"
if m := re.search(r"(\d+) items", text):
    count = int(m.group(1))
    print(f"Extracted count: {count}")   # 42

An orchestration layer for an agentic coding assistant receives tool results as raw JSON objects. Numeric fields arrive as strings when the tool output was not schema-validated. Before comparing against configured limits or building new API requests, every field must be converted explicitly.

import json
from typing import Optional

# -------------------------------------------------------------------
# Simulated tool output — all numeric fields are strings (common from
# shell-level tools or poorly typed APIs)
# -------------------------------------------------------------------
raw_tool_output = json.loads('''
{
    "exit_code": "0",
    "duration_ms": "342",
    "success": "true",
    "output_lines": "47",
    "memory_mb": "128.5",
    "error": null
}
''')

# -------------------------------------------------------------------
# Parse and validate at the boundary
# -------------------------------------------------------------------
def parse_tool_result(raw: dict) -> dict:
    exit_code: int = int(raw["exit_code"])
    duration_ms: float = float(raw["duration_ms"])
    success: bool = raw["success"].lower() == "true"
    output_lines: int = int(raw["output_lines"])
    memory_mb: float = float(raw["memory_mb"])
    error: Optional[str] = raw.get("error")   # None stays None

    return {
        "exit_code": exit_code,
        "duration_ms": duration_ms,
        "success": success,
        "output_lines": output_lines,
        "memory_mb": memory_mb,
        "error": error,
    }

result = parse_tool_result(raw_tool_output)

# -------------------------------------------------------------------
# Business logic — safe because types are now correct
# -------------------------------------------------------------------
SLOW_THRESHOLD_MS = 500.0
MAX_OUTPUT_LINES = 100

if not result["success"]:
    print(f"Tool failed with exit code {result['exit_code']}")
elif result["duration_ms"] > SLOW_THRESHOLD_MS:
    print(f"Warning: tool was slow ({result['duration_ms']:.0f} ms)")
elif result["output_lines"] > MAX_OUTPUT_LINES:
    print(f"Truncating output — {result['output_lines']} lines exceeds limit")
else:
    print(f"Tool succeeded in {result['duration_ms']:.0f} ms")

# If we had compared raw["duration_ms"] > 500 directly:
# "342" > 500  →  TypeError in Python 3; silent wrong result in Python 2
returns Python ints/floats for JSON numbers, but tool outputs serialized as strings or YAML are different stories
because non-empty strings are truthy; always parse explicitly
checks object identity, not equality; use
— floating-point representation errors make exact equality unreliable; use
silently breaks those built-ins for the rest of the scope
Re-using a name for different types
shares the same list across all calls; use
as default and create a fresh object inside the function
— Python integers are arbitrary-precision; they do not overflow; but
— HTTP, env vars, files, user input — then pass native types internally
works but is a style violation per PEP 8
Keep configuration constants at module level with
names so they are easy to locate and mock in tests
For financial or pricing calculations, use
When parsing booleans from environment or JSON strings, compare lowercased strings:
Add assertions or validation at API entry points:
assert isinstance(msg, dict), f"Expected dict, got {type(msg)}"
are C-level objects in CPython with essentially zero overhead for individual arithmetic. The cost that matters at agent scale is
— each string concatenation in a loop creates a new object. Use
or f-strings inside comprehensions rather than looping with
in a loop; collect into a list and call
— integer arithmetic is exact and very fast; floating-point introduces rounding but is equally fast in CPython
is a single identity comparison; it does not call
— it is implemented in C; calling it defensively at boundaries adds negligible overhead
— use it only where precision matters, not as a general replacement for
— CPython caches integers from −5 to 256; for these,
identity checks happen to work, but this is an implementation detail — never rely on it
— the reference static type checker; works with PEP 484 annotations to catch type mismatches before runtime
— Microsoft's type checker, faster than mypy on large codebases, integrated into VS Code
— validates and coerces data from dicts/JSON into typed models; the de-facto boundary validation library in modern agent frameworks
— runtime type enforcement via decorators; useful for catching type violations in production code during development
interactively to explore an unfamiliar object's type and methods
— ultra-fast runtime type checking via a decorator; negligible overhead compared to pydantic
Several languages handle typing differently. Understanding the contrast sharpens intuition about where Python's approach creates opportunity and where it requires discipline.
Must convert and validate at boundaries; fast to prototype
Errors caught before deploy; more boilerplate at definition
Zero implicit coercion; explicit interface satisfaction required
Similar to Python; fewer built-in numeric types
Ownership model prevents aliasing bugs; steeper learning curve

Within the Python ecosystem, the addition of type hints (PEP 484) and tools like mypy gives Python code much of the benefit of static typing without sacrificing dynamic flexibility — the preferred pattern for production agent code.

Type confusion is a real attack surface in agent systems. If an adversarially crafted tool response causes your code to treat a string as an integer or vice versa, it can bypass numeric guards, overflow limits, or inject unexpected content into prompts.
— never trust that a tool result or API response has the type your code expects; validate before use
Use pydantic models at API entry points
— they reject unexpected types and apply coercion transparently and consistently
Sanitize strings before embedding in prompts
— user-supplied or tool-generated strings can contain injection patterns; type checking alone does not prevent this
Never use eval() on model output
— even if the output looks like a Python literal; use
if you must parse Python-like syntax, and even then treat it as untrusted input
Avoid storing sensitive values in mutable shared state
— if two agent steps share a dict holding API keys, a mutation bug can expose credentials in log output
Be careful with implicit truthiness in security checks
is also False for 0, which may not be the intent
Variables are names that refer to objects; objects carry their type; the name itself is type-free
Python is dynamically typed — errors surface at runtime unless you add static analysis
Assignment creates a reference, not a copy; mutable objects shared by multiple names can surprise you
Convert external data explicitly at boundaries — never assume a string from the environment is the right type
Immutable types are safe to share; mutable types require deliberate design when passed across agent steps
; for type-safe boundary validation use pydantic

that you can attach to any box. The box (object) has a fixed color (type) and may or may not let you change its contents (mutability). A label can be moved to a different box at any time (rebinding), and two labels can be stuck to the same box (aliasing). When you "change" an immutable box, Python silently hands you a brand new box with the new value and moves your label to it — the old box still exists until nothing is pointing at it. For mutable boxes, everyone holding a label pointing at that box sees any change made through any of those labels.
